message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A ad-hoc dedicated COVID-19 hospital was setup in New Delhi, India over a span of 12 days. At this time, new teaching modalities were employed to train the staff. This study aims to identify and quantify the effectiveness of these teaching models in terms of learning which was evaluated using case scenarios before and after the teaching session.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To assess education methods and models for training of healthcare workers during rapid deployment at an ad-hoc dedicated COVID-19 hospital.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The 5E (Engage, Explore, Explain, Elaborate and Evaluate) teaching modality through peer group teaching methods was utilised in the situation. Statistical analysis was done using Mann Whitney U test.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    A total of 86 participants (43 doctors and 43 nurses) answered the pre and post test questionnaires. The number of correct responses per question in the pre test (Mean
                    <jats:underline>+</jats:underline>
                    SD;49.0
                    <jats:underline>+</jats:underline>
                    18.53) vs post test (Mean
                    <jats:underline>+</jats:underline>
                    SD;54.40
                    <jats:underline>+</jats:underline>
                    15.95) with stratification by the domain of learning was analysed. No significant difference was found in the pre and post test responses.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Further studies of this nature will contribute towards assessing efficacy of teaching modalities employed in rapid readiness for future pandemic scenarios.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: A novel 5E training model and other methods implemented for rapid readiness at an ad-hoc COVID-19 facility in India

reference.article-title: Effects of the 5E learning model on students’ academic achievements in movement and force issues

reference.unstructured: Bouadma L , Lescure FX , Lucet JC , Yazdanpanah Y , Timsit JF . 2020. Severe SARS-CoV-2 infections: practical considerations and management strategy for intensivists. Intensive Care Med.
Bybee RW . 2006. The BSCS 5E instructional model and 21st century skills. [place unknown].

reference.article-title: Evaluation of the efficacy of peer-learning method in nutrition students of Shiraz University of Medical Sciences

reference.unstructured: Karplus R. 2002. The learning cycle. In: A love Discov Sci Educ Second career Robert Karplus. [place unknown]; p. 51–76.
Singhal T. 2020. A Review of Coronavirus Disease-2019 (COVID-19). Indian J Pediatr.
